segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dramatic close-up of white granulated sugar cascading onto a dark surface, forming a mound, then spelling out 'ALZHEIMER'S', 'CANCER', and 'HEART DISEASE' to a suspenseful musical score. A female narrator's voice declares, "It's sweet. It's seductive. Is it deadly?" as the title 'THE SECRETS OF SUGAR' appears over flowing sugar, followed by the narrator announcing, "Tonight, the dangers of sugar." The scene transitions to a woman in a lab coat, who states professionally, "I think that sugar is a main contributing factor." The narrator then introduces "Serious new warnings from serious people" before a man in a suit expresses his concern, "The more I learn about it, the more it scares me." The narrator continues, "Also tonight, what the sugar industry has tried to hide," leading to an older man with a white beard and glasses revealing with an authoritative, slightly gravelly tone, "Strategies that I thought the tobacco companies made up back in the 50s, actually some of those, the sugar people had done even before that." The sound of sugar pouring intensifies with the dramatic music before the scene shifts to a family, identified by the narrator as the "Breeden family," walking into a grocery store, accompanied by light background music and faint children's voices, including one saying "Let's go!" and another making a sound like "Uh oh." Inside, the camera pans through aisles filled with sugary products, underscoring sugar's ubiquity, as the narrator explains that like most Canadians, they try to buy healthy but don't always succeed due to busy lives requiring quick meals and keeping kids happy. On-screen credits for the producer/director, writer, and associate producers appear as the family continues shopping, with a final focus on the cereal aisle, reinforcing the message about hidden sugars amidst the grocery store ambiance.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a grocery store aisle where a young girl in a white shirt is seen speaking. A man's voice asks her about cereal choices, specifically "Lucky Charms or the Mini-Wheats chocolate?" to which she replies, "No, I want a Lucky one." The man confirms, "Okay, Lucky Charms," and further inquires if she wants one that "looks like half a moon." The camera pans across brightly lit shelves, emphasizing the abundance of sugary cereals like Lucky Charms, Mini-Wheats, and Honey Nut Cheerios. A female narrator's voice-over, accompanied by light, investigative background music, explains that "a lot of what they eat is processed," and while people "assume it's nutritious," they "have no idea how much sugar is hidden in it." The scene transitions to a checkout area where a man in a blue shirt, a woman in a green shirt, and a child are placing items like Lucky Charms and eggs on the conveyor belt. The background music continues as the setting shifts to a home environment, where a woman in a light cardigan and blue top, later identified by the narrator as registered dietitian Jaclyn Pritchard, stands with the man in the blue shirt and a woman in a black tank top. Jaclyn Pritchard's clear voice initiates a discussion, asking them to "start by telling me a little bit about some of the groceries that you got today." The narrator then introduces her, stating she "is about to help them figure it out." Jaclyn asks, "Have you guys ever taken a look at any of the nutrition labels?" as she gestures toward groceries laid out on a table, including cereal boxes and a red measuring cup. The camera zooms in on a cereal box’s nutrition label, highlighting 10g of sugar and 120 calories per serving, while the narrator explains, "The label on this Nesquik cereal says there are 10 grams of sugar in three-quarters of a cup. But whoever eats just three-quarters of a cup?" Jaclyn then asks, "But how many of those would go into your bowl to make up your bowl of cereal?" The man in the blue shirt, placing a hand on his chest, responds, "For me, um... I'd say probably like eight," while the woman in the black tank top listens with a serious expression. The scene ends with Jaclyn holding the red cup, continuing the conversation in the domestic setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands holding a glass plate filled with white granulated sugar, with a banana and a bag labeled 'TOURNEZ-MOI' visible in the background, suggesting a home kitchen setting. A male voice is heard counting, then reacting, "That's a lot of cereal." The scene transitions to a Woman in cardigan, who holds the plate of sugar and speaks to a Woman in black tank top and a Man in blue shirt (home), who places a hand on his chest as if reacting to the discussion. The Woman in cardigan gestures with a red measuring cup while explaining that in about eight servings of cereal, one is "looking at about 20 teaspoons of sugar added, of non-nutritional value." The Man in blue shirt (home) reacts again, stating, "That's a lot," followed by an agreeing "Yeah" from another female voice. A distinct narrator's voice then introduces the topic of "an awful lot of added sugar." The scene cuts to a dramatic black-and-white slow-motion shot of sugar crystals falling, emphasizing their texture, accompanied by the distinct sound of pouring or rustling granules. Next, a new setting appears: a well-lit kitchen with wooden cabinets, where a Woman in beige blazer stands behind a counter displaying various sweeteners, including Redpath sugar, Coca-Cola, maple syrup, agave, and corn syrup. She gestures toward the items while speaking in an informative tone, asking, "What do we mean when we say sugar?" and explaining that whether it's "the white stuff that you bake with, or the brown stuff that you sprinkle on your oatmeal," or various syrups, "chemically, it's all pretty much the same thing." The video then cuts to a stylized black background with a row of identical sugar spoons, followed by a graphic showing '26 TEASPOONS OF SUGAR EACH DAY' and then '40 KILOS OF SUGAR EACH YEAR' with a line of sugar bags. This visual sequence is underscored by dramatic, slightly melancholic piano music, as the narrator's voice states that on average, "26 teaspoons of sugar per person, per day," which is "40 kilos a year," or "the equivalent of 20 bags." The final shot is a close-up of a Nestlé Nesquik cereal box, featuring a cartoon rabbit holding a spoonful of cereal, with the brand name prominently displayed in bold blue and white letters.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a package of Schneider's Juicy Jumbo Beef Davioli hot dogs, followed by a rapid montage of iconic food brand logos including General Mills, Kraft, Heinz, Kellogg’s, and Nutella, all presented with vibrant clarity. Throughout this, a female narrator's voice, accompanied by a slightly suspenseful, investigative background score, explains how these powerful and familiar companies sweeten products and spike profits, noting that the food industry is a trillion-dollar business in North America that couldn't exist without sugar. The scene then shifts to Bruce Bradley, a bald man with glasses in a light blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera against a soft blue background. A graphic overlay identifies him as a former food industry executive with experience at Pillsbury, Nabisco, and General Mills. Bruce Bradley, speaking in a calm, clear male voice, emphasizes that sugar is one of the "essential basic ingredients used in 99% of the processed foods out there" and a "basic building block" that drives a lot of taste and appeal for consumers. The female narrator then explicitly introduces him as "former industry executive Bruce Bradley." The video transitions to brightly lit grocery store aisles, first showcasing a variety of yogurt brands like iögo, Activia, and Yoplait, then moving to an aisle stocked with frozen pizza products such as McCain Pizza Pockets and Pizza Pops. The narrator concludes by stating that the amount of sugar in food is no accident, as the industry meticulously figures out the "exact combination of ingredients it calls the bliss point" to make consumers crave a product.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a visual tour of a grocery store frozen pizza aisle, showcasing various products like McCain Pizza Pockets and Pizza Minis with visible price tags, as a male voice (Dr. Howard Moskowitz) immediately poses the question, "everybody asks, what is the bliss point?" A graphic then introduces Dr. Howard Moskowitz, nicknamed 'Dr. Bliss' and identified as a 'Food Industry Consultant,' accompanied by a subtle sound effect. The scene transitions to Dr. Moskowitz in a live interview, speaking animatedly against a soft blue background. He explains the 'bliss point' concept using an analogy of adding milk to coffee, describing how there's an optimal amount where you like it the most, beyond which it becomes "too milky" or "horrid." He defines it as the "Goldilocks" level, "the middle, it's the best one, it's the level where you like the product the most." As a graphical representation of a 'BLISS POINT' chart appears, illustrating optimal ingredient ratios, a female narrator with a clear voice explains that Moskowitz, a "Harvard-trained mathematician," uses models to test reactions to product versions. Light, scientific-sounding background music begins, continuing as the narrator concludes that once the bliss point is found, "the product hits the shelves," and "his magic makes money," from soda pop to spaghetti sauce.

The chart visually illustrates the concept of achieving maximum consumer satisfaction through precise ingredient ratios, tying back to Dr. Moskowitz’s expertise in food product development.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Dr. Howard Moskowitz, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a black shirt, speaking in an interview setting against a soft blue background. With a clear, articulate male voice, he explains how the food industry adds "a little sugar" to products to increase acceptance. The scene then transitions to a black screen where a measuring spoon filled with white granulated sugar is shown next to a sugar cube, visually equating '4 GRAMS = 1 TEASPOON'. A calm, informative female narrator questions "A little?" before explaining this conversion, accompanied by light, upbeat background music. A can of Coca-Cola then appears beside the spoon, with the text '10 TEASPOONS' highlighting its sugar content, as the narrator states it contains "40 grams a can, that's 10 teaspoons." The video cuts to a cluttered display of various processed food products, including Kraft BBQ sauce, Hamburger Helper, Campbell’s tomato soup, and Redpath sugar, emphasizing hidden sugars, as the narrator explains that much of the sugar we eat is "hidden in foods we don't necessarily think of as sweet." Close-ups of nutrition labels follow, starting with a Quaker Oatmeal box, which the narrator identifies as having "three and three-quarter teaspoons of sugar a bowl" (14g sugars). Next, a Liberté Greek Yogurt container (19g sugars) is shown, with the narrator pointing out it contains "nearly five teaspoons in just half a cup." The narrator then broadly states, "You can find sugar added to bread, soup, all kinds of condiments, hot dogs," as the visuals show a Wonder bread package (15g sugars) and Schneider’s Juicy Jumbo hot dogs (14g sugars). The final product shown is a Healthy Choice Pineapple Chicken meal, with its label revealing 22g of sugars, which the narrator highlights as having "five and a half teaspoons of sugar in every serving." The segment ends with a brief outdoor shot of a man in a plaid shirt walking with a child, suggesting real-world implications of sugar consumption, as the light background music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with real-world street scenes featuring overweight individuals, including a man in a checkered shirt and khaki pants, a woman in a green and white striped top holding a child’s hand, and two men standing near a trash can. A female voice states, "This is the result. There's no question as our consumption of sugars has grown, so have our bodies," and mentions using American statistics due to a lack of Canadian data. The visuals then transition to a stark black background with white line drawings illustrating human evolution, culminating in a modern, obese figure, as the voice asks, "Are we changing our evolutionary shape?" A red line labeled 'SUGAR' appears, rising sharply from 1960 to 2009, which the narrator identifies as "our sugar consumption for the last 50 years." This is followed by parallel rising blue, green, and yellow lines, identified by the voice as "the number of people who've become overweight and obese," "cases of type 2 diabetes," and "diseases of the heart," respectively. The scene then shifts to a well-lit kitchen setting where a Woman in a beige blazer, with blonde hair, speaks directly to the camera with a serious and concerned expression. She explains that in the 80s and 90s, "we used to blame a lot of those problems on dietary fat," but questions if removing fat helped, noting that "the incidence of disease" did not go down. She concludes that this led doctors and nutritionists to ask "why," revealing that "the answer, according to an increasingly vocal group, is sugar," as the video ends on a close-up of a textured, grayish surface. Throughout the segment, a subtle, serious background music track plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Dr. Robert Lustig, a man with graying hair in a dark suit, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit room. His clear, authoritative male voice asks, "Which was worse? The sugar or the fat? The sugar, a thousand times over." An upbeat, slightly dramatic background music begins as the scene transitions with a whoosh sound to a stylized nutrition label graphic introducing Dr. Lustig as a 'Pediatric Endocrinologist,' 'Author: Fat Chance,' and 'Medical Professor.' A female narrator's voice identifies him as "Robert Lustig, doctor, author, medical professor, and one of the leaders of the anti-sugar campaign." The video cuts back to Dr. Lustig, who states that "our food supply has been altered and adulterated under our very noses and in plain sight over the past 30 years." A tablet then displays a presentation titled 'Sugar: The Bitter Truth' from UCSF, followed by Dr. Lustig at a podium in a lecture hall, delivering a talk with blue slides discussing 'The perfect storm from three political winds' and 'Adulteration of our food supply,' including points on fructose, fiber removal, and trans fats. The female narrator returns, highlighting that "Lustig is a YouTube sensation" whose lecture on sugar has been seen by nearly 4 million people, adding "he doesn't pull his punches." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Dr. Robert Lustig, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and white shirt, speaking earnestly in a softly lit room with vertical blinds, explaining that while many things have 'empty calories,' not all are 'poisonous.' Upbeat, slightly mysterious background music begins as a female narrator takes over, stating that Dr. Lustig calls sugar 'poisonous' due to its effects on the body. The visuals transition to a close-up of a glass mug of dark, bubbling soda, emphasizing carbonation, before moving to an animated sequence. This animation depicts two textured spheres, one blue (representing glucose) and one pink (representing fructose), interacting with gray spheres, illustrating molecular processes. The narrator explains that sugar comprises these two molecules, glucose and fructose. The animation then shifts to a translucent human torso, highlighting internal organs, with the liver illuminated in bright pink, as the narrator details how glucose circulates to muscles and the brain, while fructose goes directly to the liver, where 'all kinds of problems begin.' The scene cuts back to Dr. Lustig, who continues his explanation, stating that metabolizing 'fructose in excess' forces the liver to convert that energy into 'liver fat,' leading to 'downstream metabolic diseases.' The female narrator returns, promising more on these diseases but first shifting focus to the brain, as the segment concludes with a young boy with curly hair, wearing a black headband and plaid shirt, casually drinking from a cup with a straw and eating a sandwich, visually connecting the scientific discussion to real-world consumption, particularly by children.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a young boy with curly brown hair, wearing a black headband and a plaid shirt, enthusiastically eating a sandwich in a casual dining setting. Over this, Dr. Robert Lustig's voice explains how "fructose... shuts down the part of your brain that tells you when you're full," and that it "doesn't get registered by the brain as you're having eaten." The scene then cuts to Dr. Lustig, a man with graying hair in a dark suit, speaking earnestly in a softly lit indoor environment, continuing to elaborate that if you "take a kid and prep them with a soft drink and then let them loose at the fast food restaurant," they will eat more. Upbeat, slightly dramatic background music begins as the video transitions to Phyllis Tanaka, a woman with short brown hair, glasses, a black blazer, and a red beaded necklace, speaking with expressive hand gestures. Her voice states, "I think there's a long way to go before the literature is sorted out." A graphic overlay identifies her as 'Phyllis Tanaka' representing 'Food and Consumer Products Canada' (PepsiCo, General Mills, Kraft). A female narrator's voice then interjects, explaining that Phyllis "speaks for the biggest food companies in Canada," and "doesn't buy Dr. Lustig's theories and doesn't think consumers should either." Phyllis Tanaka's voice returns, emphasizing, "I think it's more important that we step back and look at how do we... look for ways to educate and help consumers fit sugar into a healthy dietary pattern." The narrator's voice concludes, "But the industry sure doesn't make it easy. Look at this breakfast bar," as the video displays a yellow box of belVita Breakfast Oatmeal Crunch cereal biscuits, highlighting its branding and bilingual labeling, before zooming in on its ingredients list, which prominently features whole grain oats, rye flour, canola oil, sugar, evaporated cane juice, and corn syrup, among other additives.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with dramatic, suspenseful synth music accompanying a black-and-white animation of falling sugar granules and the text 'THE SECRETS OF SUGAR will return,' setting a serious tone. This transitions to a black screen displaying a Tropicana orange juice carton alongside a whole orange, with the title 'Fruits vs Fruit juice' and a URL for sugar facts, suggesting a comparison of natural versus processed sugar sources. The music then fades, replaced by subtle mechanical whirring and distinct thudding sounds as the scene shifts from a suburban street to a modest home kitchen. Here, a man in a black shirt opens a refrigerator, carefully examining labels on various condiments and beverages, including a Heinz Tomato Sauce bottle. He audibly notes the sugar content, stating "2 grams of sugar" and later "1 gram of sugar" for a "Craft Zesty Italian" product. A female narrator explains that "Having discovered just how much sugar is in their food, the Breedon family is on a purge," adding that they are "still surprised at the kinds of products that contain sugar," with a faint mention of "ketchup" in the background. The man gathers several packaged sugary foods, including Lucky Charms cereal and Nesquik, into a clear plastic bin. The narrator continues, "But they're also determined. All of it, out it goes." The man and a woman in a blue t-shirt with a large white '4' then carry the bin to a metal garbage chute in a tiled hallway, where they dispose of the items one by one, accompanied by distinct thudding and clattering sounds. The narrator concludes, "Of course, they still have to eat. So to help them learn about life beyond processed foods, we've made them a deal. For three weeks, we'll provide all of their meals professionally made without any added sugar. They'll stick to the diet and submit..." as the sounds of discarded items continue until the end of the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a hand placing a dark object into a metal garbage chute, as a narrator introduces the segment about "medical tests." Jonathan, a man with curly dark hair in a black shirt, is then seen in a cluttered hallway, looking down and speaking, remarking, "Lucky charms ain't working anymore." The scene transitions to Anna, a woman with dark hair tied back, wearing glasses and a gray t-shirt, standing in a room with clothes and a closet in the background. Environmental sounds of traffic are faintly audible. Five-year-old Ruby, a young girl in a pink top and gray pants, steps onto a white scale with pink footpads, while Jonathan stands nearby. The narrator explains that "according to medical standards, both Jonathan and Anna are technically obese," and Ruby is "hovering on the edge." The video then shows an outdoor street view, displaying a sign for the 'Filipino Centre, Toronto' and a 'Medicare Drug Mart' with a phone number. Inside a medical facility, a woman in a white lab coat draws blood from Ruby’s arm, with Jonathan holding the child. The narrator reveals, "We started our experiment by having their blood tested and analyzed by obesity specialist Dr. Dan Flanders," adding that "The family he says is heading for trouble." A close-up of a computer screen displays detailed blood test results, highlighting values like HEMOGLOBIN A1C, TRIGLYCERIDES, and GLUCOSE. Dr. Dan Flanders, a man with short dark hair and glasses in a white collared shirt, is seated at a desk, reviewing these results before turning to speak directly to the camera. He expresses his deep concern, stating, "Looking at these results, I would say that I'm very concerned. Quite frankly, if they don't make meaningful change to their lifestyle relatively soon, um, there's a higher chance that they're heading for a life of lousy quality of life and early death." A phone rings in the background as the narrator concludes, "Like most of us, getting fatter and sicker. The Britains..." The final scene shows a reception area with a 'Toronto Medical Clinic' sign and a family waiting in the background, with a clipboard and pen in the foreground.

A young girl in a pink top and gray pants steps onto a scale, while a man in a black shirt stands nearby. The setting changes to an outdoor street view, showing a sign for the 'Filipino Centre, Toronto' and a 'Medicare Drug Mart' with a phone number. Inside a medical facility, a woman in a white lab coat draws blood from a child’s arm, while a man in a black shirt holds the child. The video then shows a close-up of a blood test result on a computer screen, highlighting values like HEMOGLOBIN A1C, TRIGLYCERIDES, and GLUCOSE. A man in a white shirt and glasses, seated at a desk, reviews the results on a computer, then turns to speak directly to the camera. The final scene shows a reception area with a sign for 'Toronto Medical Clinic' and a family waiting in the background, with a clipboard and pen in the foreground.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens in a dimly lit medical clinic waiting area with a checkered floor, where a family—a woman in a black top and glasses, a man in a dark hoodie with a backpack, and two young children (one in a purple shirt, the other in a blue shirt in a stroller)—is seated. The man interacts with the child in the stroller as the woman looks on, while a male narrator's voice, accompanied by a subtle, dramatic background score, discusses how people might be forgiven their 'nutritional ignorance,' but the food industry has known about links between processed food and disease for decades. The scene then transitions to a black-and-white, first-person perspective of a car driving through a city street, passing under an overpass and entering a parking garage, with the distinct sounds of a car engine and driving. The camera follows the car as it navigates the dimly lit garage, eventually stopping with a 'clunk' sound, and the driver exiting. The narrator continues, detailing a 1999 Minneapolis meeting where 'America's biggest food companies' like Kraft, Nabisco, Nestle, Coca-Cola, and General Mills convened as 'obesity' was an 'emerging problem.' The video then shifts to a close-up of Michael Moss, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a brightly lit indoor setting with a red wall. A graphic overlay identifies him as 'New York Times Reporter Michael Moss, Author: Sugar, Salt, & Fat.' Michael Moss articulately explains how these executives, who normally 'are biting each other for space on the grocery store,' rarely meet but gathered in '99 to talk about 'obesity.' The narrator briefly returns to identify him as 'Reporter and author, Michael Moss,' who described this meeting in his best-selling book, before Michael Moss concludes by stating they 'had been pulled together.'

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dramatized scene of a man, identified as James O. Hill, Ph.D., in a dark suit at a podium, speaking in front of a blue projection screen titled 'Responding to the Challenge of Childhood Obesity.' A deep-voiced male narrator (Narrator 1) immediately sets a serious tone, discussing the 'food industry' and a 'slippery slope,' visually reinforced by a 'CAUTION SLIPPERY SLOPE' sign. As the narrator continues, imagining a 'distinct sliding sensation,' the video transitions to presentation slides. A low, sustained, slightly ominous string sound begins as background music. A female narrator (Narrator 2) then highlights how 'graphics drove home the point,' as maps of the United States visually demonstrate rising obesity rates in 1985, 1991, and 1997, spreading 'across the country like a rash.' A third male voice (Narrator 3) then asks, 'What are the health implications of all this?' as a 'Consequences of Obesity' slide appears, detailing chronic diseases like diabetes, heart disease, hypertension, and cancer, which the narrator explains are higher risks for obese individuals. Narrator 2 returns, identifying 'the ubiquity of inexpensive, good-tasting, super-sized, energy-dense foods' as a top contributing factor, aligning with the 'An Environment That Makes Obesity Easy' slide, adding that these were 'the very foods the CEOs were in charge of selling.' The segment concludes with Narrator 2 stating that 'the two men' (referring to the visually presented men with mustaches, one in a patterned tie, the other in a blue shirt, speaking in different settings) were hoping for funding to study the link between food and obesity but instead received a 'tongue-lashing,' specifically mentioning Steven Sanger, the head of General Mills. The overall tone remains serious and informative, using visual aids and multiple narrators to emphasize the growing public health crisis.

The next slide details the 'Consequences of Obesity,' listing conditions like Type 2 Diabetes, CHD, and Cancer, along with percentages of cases attributable to obesity and associated direct and indirect costs. A subsequent slide outlines 'An Environment That Makes Obesity Easy,' highlighting factors such as the ubiquity of inexpensive, energy-dense foods, decline in physical activity, and increase in sedentary activities. The video then cuts to a man with a mustache, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking in a different setting, followed by a close-up of another man with a mustache, wearing a dark suit and blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a brightly lit indoor environment with a red wall in the background. The overall tone is serious and informative, using visual aids to emphasize the growing public health crisis of obesity and its societal and economic impacts.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Moss, a man with graying hair in a dark suit, speaking directly to the camera against a red wall. He seriously explains how "he was rather furious at Mudd" for blaming them, and how the industry defends itself by stating they "already offer consumers a choice" for low-fat or low-sugar products, feeling responsible to both consumers and Wall Street. The scene then cuts to a woman with blonde hair in a beige blazer, standing in a kitchen filled with processed foods like Kraft BBQ sauce and Campbell’s soup. She gestures as she states, "In other words, they didn't want to know," detailing how troublesome voices are silenced and how "Michael Mudd eventually left the food industry out of frustration." She adds that those profiting from sugar are "very adept at crushing dissenting voices everywhere, including in the halls of science." A black-and-white, vintage-style countdown graphic with a circular target and the number '3' appears, accompanied by a distinct ticking sound effect that culminates in a 'ding'. Finally, an older man with glasses, dressed in a dark suit, speaks calmly and authoritatively in a monochrome setting, beginning to describe how things are "increasingly" in front of us before the segment concludes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of a man in glasses and a suit, speaking earnestly in an archival interview, his voice slightly muffled as he refers to 'very tempting food'. The scene then transitions to a modern, warmly lit living room where a man in a blue shirt is seated at a small round table, sorting through books. He picks up and examines 'Pure, White and Deadly' by John Yudkin, its subtitle 'How Sugar Is Killing Us and What We Can Do to Stop It' prominently displayed. A female narrator introduces John Yudkin as a British nutritionist whose 1972 book, 'Pure, White and Deadly,' was a culmination of decades of research leading to controversial conclusions disliked by the sugar industry. A woman in a red top sits beside the man in blue, listening attentively as he speaks directly to the camera, explaining that Yudkin began to wonder in the late 1950s if sugar was a culprit in the increase of heart disease. The woman then asks if this was 'more significant than fat,' to which the man in blue responds, emphasizing that sugar was 'certainly more significant than fat' and also involved in other undesirable conditions like diabetes and obesity. The background features a fireplace and framed pictures. The video cuts to a black-and-white portrait of a smiling John Yudkin, followed by a similar portrait of another man, identified by the narrator as American nutritionist Ancel Keys, who was 'big sugar's biggest apologist' and later exposed for being funded by the industry.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white portrait of a man with glasses and neatly combed hair, wearing a sweater, as a male narrator's voice, accompanied by dramatic background music, discusses an 'industry' and the destruction of 'John Yudkin's reputation.' The scene transitions to Science Writer Gary Taubes, identified by a nutrition-label-style graphic, speaking in a modern interview setting. He explains that as early as the 1950s, someone had started producing publications suggesting 'dietary fat was a problem.' The narrator then formally introduces 'Award-winning science writer and author, Gary Taubes,' who continues to detail how someone 'successfully managed to taint Yudkin with this smell of quackery,' leading to anyone researching sugar being accused of being 'just like Yudkin.' The video cuts to a black-and-white historical photograph of a bald man with glasses, wearing a suit and tie, seated at a desk and holding a book titled 'This Slimming Business,' as another male voice describes a 'systematic campaign to discredit or ignore his work.' The scene returns to Gary Taubes, who elaborates on how the 'actions of the sugar industry in the 70s' resulted in virtually no research being funded, reinforcing the idea that studying sugar made one a 'quack.' A woman with blonde hair, wearing a black top with a pink collar, is shown listening intently beside Gary Taubes, then her voice is heard asking if 'scientific investigation into the link between sugar and disease, ground to a halt?' to which Gary Taubes confirms, 'ground to a halt.' The final scene shows a laboratory setting with a woman in a white lab coat and gloves handling test tubes labeled 'DRS 24hr,' with a red biohazard bin and labeled trays visible, as the background music swells dramatically.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a hand pouring a dark, amber-colored liquid into a clear glass, as a male voice declares, "The science is back." This voice continues, asking, "What happens when you take healthy students and feed them too much sugar?" while the scene transitions to a hospital setting. Here, a woman in a green and black checkered shirt serves a meal tray to a patient in a black tank top, who sips orange juice from a glass. Dramatic, slightly ominous music begins as a black-and-white shot shows sugar granules cascading, overlaid with the text 'THE SECRETS OF SUGAR will return.' A large white question mark appears, followed by 'What's your recommended daily limit for sugar?' and the URL 'Sugar Facts: cbc.ca/fifth,' suggesting an educational theme. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a hand holding a FreeStyle glucose meter, displaying a reading of 121 mg/dL. Over subtle background music, a female narrator's voice mentions "175 countries with people's diets." The scene then transitions to Dr. Robert Lustig, a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit indoor setting. He asks, "When you adjust for all of the factors that we know are relevant, what about the food supply predicts diabetes rates worldwide?" and emphatically states the "Answer: Sugar. And only sugar." A brief transition sound leads to Dr. John Sievenpiper, a nutrition researcher from St. Michael’s Hospital, Toronto, introduced by a stylized graphic and the narrator. Visually, he appears in a light blue shirt and dark tie, speaking with animated gestures against a warm-toned background. Dr. Sievenpiper's voice explains that "These studies are generally considered a very weak level of evidence. A lot of other things have happened at the same time." The narrator then clarifies that "He argues Lustig's methodology is seriously flawed." Dr. Sievenpiper continues, detailing how "methodologists would tell you there's a lot of potential bias," giving an example of how "over the same time as sugar has gone up, so has bottled water," but there's "no real biological plausibility" linking bottled water to obesity. He concludes that we must be "careful in putting too much of the biological plausibility and wanting to believe patterns that we see." The narrator summarizes his point: "it's hard to know what causes disease." The segment concludes with a brief, blurred shot of pedestrians walking in an urban environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a nameplate reading 'Lewis C. Camery, Dream Team Leader' on a conference table, alongside a microphone and water bottle, as a female narrator introduces a 'scientific dream team' of 'America's top cancer specialists.' The camera then pans to show several individuals seated at the table, including a man in a dark suit gesturing emphatically while speaking into a microphone, as the narrator continues to explain their mission to 'supercharge the search for a cure' and mentions that 'when Cantley talks, people listen.' The scene transitions to a whiteboard filled with handwritten notes and diagrams, including phrases like 'For all cancer patients' and 'Faster Medicine,' where three men in business attire stand, one gesturing as if explaining a concept. The focus then shifts to a close-up of a man with a mustache and beard, wearing a dark suit and blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit interview setting. A female interviewer's voice asks, 'do you believe that sugar consumption causes cancer?' The man on screen, presumably Cantley, responds in a calm, authoritative voice, stating, 'I think yes, I think that eating too much sugar can definitely increase the probability of cancer and also make the outcome of people who already have cancer out worse.' The female narrator returns, asking 'So how?' as animated graphics begin, showing two textured spheres merging, accompanied by an upbeat, slightly dramatic background music. The narrator explains that sugar is made of 'one molecule glucose and one fructose.' The animation then transitions to a translucent human torso with the liver and pancreas highlighted in bright pink, illustrating their anatomical location. The narrator continues to explain how 'too much fructose in the liver' sets off a chain reaction, causing the pancreas to produce 'more insulin,' and how 'Cantley now believes' this excess insulin 'changes cancer tumors,' telling them to 'gobble up the glucose,' before the scene fades to black, with the background music continuing.

The scene then transitions to a laboratory environment where the same man, now standing at a lab bench, points to a computer monitor displaying two side-by-side medical scans labeled 'PI3K inhibitor BYL719 phase I study' with dates '9/21/2011' and '10/24/2011'. The scans show a human torso with dark spots indicating tumor activity, suggesting a before-and-after comparison of treatment efficacy. The lab is well-equipped with wooden cabinets, scientific instruments, and a sink, and another person in a red and white lab coat is partially visible in the foreground. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man with dark hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit and tie, speaking with a serious expression, stating he "certainly avoid[s] sugar when I can." As the word 'ALZHEIMER'S' dramatically appears in white, cloud-like text over the screen, a dramatic, slightly ominous background music begins. The scene transitions to a laboratory setting where a researcher, a young man with dark hair and glasses, in a white lab coat and blue gloves, handles a transparent container labeled 'Nitrosamine' and 'Control'. A female narrator's voice explains, "One of the criticisms of the anti-sugar scientists is that too much of their evidence comes from animals, not humans," adding that at Brown University, studies "should make a lot of humans nervous." The researcher carefully places a small black mouse into a water maze, a circular tank filled with opaque white liquid, where the mouse swims and searches for a hidden platform, accompanied by the sound of splashing water. The narrator describes a "perfectly healthy" rat finding its way to safety. The camera follows the mouse’s movements as it navigates the maze, occasionally pausing to observe its behavior, while the researcher takes notes on a clipboard, and a male voice is heard stating "5.2," possibly a data point. The narrator then contrasts this with another mouse, implying it has consumed a "North American diet" of fats and sugars, and "doesn't know where to go," its "brain has been damaged." The scene cuts to a woman with short curly dark hair, wearing a light blue lab coat and dangling earrings, speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit laboratory environment, who states, "These rats were totally normal, and then they turned into demented animals." The video returns to the water maze, showing the mouse continuing its search, reinforcing the scientific testing of cognitive function in relation to chemical exposure and diet, with the overall atmosphere remaining clinical and methodical, emphasizing research and observation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a black mouse swimming in a circular water maze filled with opaque white liquid, actively searching for and successfully climbing onto a hidden white platform, demonstrating cognitive navigation. A male voice explains this behavior, noting that “they're learning after even a day,” and as the challenge increases, “they fail more and more, just like a human with Alzheimer's disease.” A researcher in a white lab coat and blue gloves is then seen observing the mouse's behavior, while a different male voice states “36.2,” possibly a data reading. The scene transitions to a female researcher in a white lab coat and purple gloves using a hairdryer to dry a mouse inside a transparent enclosure. A narrator's voice introduces the lab's belief that “Alzheimer's is really diabetes of the brain, linked to insulin levels, which can be affected by too much sugar,” before introducing “Professor Suzanne de la Monte.” Professor de la Monte, a woman in a lab coat with short curly dark hair, then speaks directly to the camera in a laboratory setting, explaining that “insulin resistance... can occur in any organ,” detailing its manifestation in “muscles” (diabetes), “liver” (fatty liver disease), “ovaries” (polycystic ovary disease), and crucially, “in the brain, and we think that's Alzheimer's.” The final scene shifts to a woman with blonde hair, wearing a beige blazer over a pink shirt, standing in a kitchen with various food items on the counter. A narrator's voice clarifies that “none of this research represents the scientific mainstream,” stating that “the case against sugar has not been proven,” and mentions “associations on both sides of the border for Alzheimer's, cancer, diabetes.” The lighting is bright and clinical in the lab scenes, while the kitchen scene has a warmer, domestic ambiance.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with the Woman in beige blazer, serving as the segment's narrator, standing in a cluttered kitchen and speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, discussing how Health Canada and the FDA haven't warned about links between sugar and disease, despite research. She then introduces an important group raising the alarm. The scene transitions to animated graphics, accompanied by light, slightly dramatic background music, illustrating the American Heart Association's recommendations: a newspaper headline from 'Matters of the Heart' urging a daily limit on added sugar, followed by sugar cubes with a heart symbol, a measuring spoon filled with sugar, and text indicating '6 TEASPOONS PER DAY WOMEN' and '9 TEASPOONS PER DAY MEN'. The graphics culminate in '26 TEASPOONS PER DAY' with sugar cascading from the spoon, accompanied by a distinct whoosh/rattle sound, as the narrator highlights the average sugar intake. The narrator then notes the Canadian food industry remains unimpressed. The video cuts to an interview setting, a warmly lit room with a fireplace and framed artwork, where the Woman in beige blazer is now seated across from Phyllis Tanaka. Phyllis, wearing a black blazer, red beaded necklace, and glasses, speaks with conviction, detailing how people are convinced of a correlation between sugar and various health issues like diabetes, heart disease, cancer, and dementia. The Woman in beige blazer listens intently, occasionally nodding and making an affirmative "Mhm" sound, as Phyllis questions the implications if these people are right.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a serious Woman in a beige blazer in an interview setting, as Phyllis Tanaka, wearing glasses and a red beaded necklace, expressively states that "the science just isn't there to support a role in chronic disease." A male narrator then announces, "When we come back, government goes on the attack," accompanied by dramatic music. The scene shifts to a hospital, showing a child's bandaged foot, followed by a man in a blue shirt drinking soda, visually surrounded by multiple 5-pound sugar bags. A text overlay and the narrator emphasize, "The equivalent of 50 pounds of sugar from just 1 soda a day," before the narrator adds, "And big sugar strikes back," as the music becomes more intense. Upbeat, circus-like music plays as the video quickly cuts through a young boy happily eating a green ice cream cone outdoors, a woman in a green top at a restaurant table, and a close-up of sugar being poured, introducing the title 'THE SECRETS OF SUGAR'. The visual narrative continues with a factory conveyor belt moving orange juice cartons and close-ups of 'SUGAR' bags on a shelf, while the music transitions to an ominous, suspenseful tone. A female narrator then begins to speak, stating, "Worldwide, there are few industries more powerful than the processed food industry, or the sugar industry that feeds it. And yet, for all their power, we know remarkably little about how they work." The music intensifies with a liquid pouring sound, leading to the final scene of a Woman with glasses, wearing a blue cardigan, working intently at a laptop in a library, surrounded by red folders and bookshelves, as the narrator identifies her: "Kristen Cousins is determined to change that."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman, identified by a narrator as a pediatric dentist in Colorado, with blonde, wavy hair and glasses, wearing a blue blazer, seated at a wooden desk in a library. She is deeply focused on her work, flipping through documents and typing on a laptop, with bookshelves filled with books in the background. The narrator explains that this woman had unearthed a stash of documents from a defunct sugar company, giving her a peek into a very secretive world. The camera then cuts to a close-up of a confidential letter from 'The Sugar Association, Inc.', dated July 17, 1975, addressed to 'THE PUBLIC COMMUNICATIONS COMMITTEE,' revealing that the association funded research on sugar and planned to distribute a press packet including a paper titled 'Sugar in the Diet of Man.' The letter explicitly states that the association paid for research time and reprints, and that they did not consider the papers compromised by their funding. The woman with glasses then speaks, describing her discovery: "The first folder that I pulled out opened up to a memo, the blue letterhead of the Sugar Association, and it had the word confidential underneath the letterhead. And I just looked at that and I, oh my god, you know, what have I found?" The scene returns to her, her expression thoughtful and engaged as she analyzes the document. The narrator elaborates that she had found a directive from the 70s, a memo to industry executives about a newly published scientific white paper concluding sugar was not only safe but important. The woman with glasses further explains her findings, stating, "It was clear after reading further that the Sugar Association had funded this white paper called Sugar in the Diet of Man, and they were trying to make it appear that it was an independent study." The camera then shifts to show her sitting across from another woman with blonde hair, wearing a beige blazer, both reviewing documents at a long wooden table in the scholarly, warm-lit library, surrounded by stacks of red folders and books, suggesting a collaborative research effort in a quiet, focused environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a quiet, well-lit library where the Woman with glasses, wearing a blue blazer, and the Woman in beige blazer are seated at a wooden table, deeply engaged in reviewing documents. The Woman with glasses is heard speaking, detailing her discovery of "more than 1500 pages," including "Canadian ones," and an "account of a sugar industry meeting in the 70s in Montreal that included frank talk about heart disease." A close-up reveals text discussing the 'greatest threat to sugar consumption' and its 'influence in atherosclerosis,' which the Woman with glasses reads aloud. She then states, "So they were worried," to which the Woman in beige blazer (or another female voice) echoes, "They were worried," and asks, "What does that say to you?" The Woman with glasses responds, "They've known for a long time," and explains her research method: she "crossed out where it said tobacco and put in sugar" to find "similar tactics that the sugar industry was using." The scene transitions to a conference room where the Woman with glasses now stands at the head of a long table, presenting to a group of attentive professionals. She gestures toward a large projection screen displaying 'SUGAR LIMIT' in bold black letters. The audience, including the Older man with white beard and a Woman in green top, takes notes and observes in a professional, academic atmosphere. A narrator's voice identifies the Woman with glasses as "Cousins," pursuing her research at the "University of California in San Francisco," under the tutelage of someone.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Stan Glantz, an older man with curly white hair, a full white beard, and glasses, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned brown tie. He speaks animatedly with a clear, slightly gravelly voice, explaining "the amazing thing I learned from her" about how strategies used by tobacco companies in the 1950s were actually predated by the sugar industry, mentioning "83 million pages of industry documents" available online. A female voice then introduces him as Stan Glantz, famous for publicizing secret tobacco industry documents that revealed companies knew their product was dangerous, and notes that he sees many parallels in new sugar documents. As the female narrator speaks, the scene transitions to Glantz opening a heavy-duty metal door, revealing a vast archive room. He then walks through a narrow aisle flanked by tall shelves packed with white archival boxes, accompanied by a woman with blonde hair in a beige blazer, as they converse. The camera cuts back to Glantz, who continues to speak about these parallels, describing efforts to "undermine science" and "attack and intimidate scientists," and "subvert sensible regulation," while the woman listens intently. The female narrator concludes by stating the sugar industry has "decades of practice in that," as the video ends with a wide, serene shot of a lakeside cityscape with white buildings, green trees, and mountains.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a formal World Health Organization (WHO) conference hall in Geneva, where delegates, many wearing headphones, are seated at long wooden tables under a prominent blue banner with the WHO emblem. A female narrator explains that in 2003, the WHO was considering a resolution to reduce sugar intake, which had broad appeal among health experts. The scene transitions to a man with graying hair in a light blue shirt, who, speaking earnestly with a clear American accent, describes how the sugar industry then lobbied US Congress, getting influential congressmen to write letters deeming the resolution unacceptable and threatening to pull US funding for the World Health Organization if the report continued. This is followed by a brief shot of a woman with blonde hair listening intently, before cutting back to the bustling conference hall. The narrator then reveals that five months later, the recommendation quietly disappeared. The video then shifts to a modern archive space where an older man with curly white hair and a beard, identified by the narrator as Stan Glantz, walks towards a glass-enclosed display titled 'CIGARETTE PAPERS', featuring historical tobacco company quotes, including a 1954 statement denying health risks. Stan Glantz examines the exhibit, then turns to the camera, gesturing expressively as he thoughtfully states, with a clear American accent, that we wouldn't have a tobacco epidemic without a tobacco industry, just as we wouldn't have an obesity epidemic without an industry making a lot of money selling sugar. Throughout the segment, a consistent background music track plays, accompanying the narration and interviews.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of an Older man with white beard, wearing a dark suit and patterned brown tie, speaking expressively in a softly lit indoor setting. He states that 'fat and salt and things like that' are issues, and that 'the bottom line is that one of the key disease vectors for non-communicable diseases is big corporations,' which 'we're going to have to get... under control.' A whoosh sound accompanies a cut to a Woman in a red top listening intently. The scene then cuts back to the Older man with white beard, but a different male voice, later identified as Bruce Bradley, begins to speak, noting that 'It has become a bit of a moral issue' and reflecting on 'how far we've come.' The video then cuts to Bruce Bradley, a bald man with glasses in a light blue collared shirt, speaking against a soft blue background, with a graphic overlay identifying him as a 'Former Food Industry Executive' from Pillsbury, Nabisco, and General Mills. A female narrator confirms, 'Bruce Bradley, who used to help run a number of those corporations, agrees.' Bruce Bradley continues, asserting, 'This isn't a blip. This isn't a minor... course correction. We're on a completely wrong trajectory with our health.' A female voice asks, 'What's the answer then?' as the camera briefly shows a Woman in a beige blazer listening with a serious expression. Bruce Bradley responds, 'I think the honest answer is that we need government to step in and to become an advocate for consumers.' Another whoosh sound transitions the scene to a close-up of a hand placing a green soda bottle into a refrigerator, followed by a Child in a green shirt eating a sandwich. The female narrator states, 'But sugary drinks are a big reason for the...' and continues over a shot of an elderly Woman in a blue and white striped dress sitting on a park bench with a cane, saying, 'But look what's happened when governments have tried. Earlier this year, New York City passed a law banning supersized sugar...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a blue shirt drinking from a soda bottle, as a female narrator explains that "If your kids drink one bottle of soda a day, they're eating the equivalent of 50 pounds of sugar a year." This is dramatically illustrated by multiple 5-pound sugar bags appearing on a table, accompanied by a pink text overlay stating 'The equivalent of 50 pounds of sugar from just 1 soda a day.' The scene transitions to a satirical magazine cover titled 'The Nanny,' featuring a man in a purple outfit, labeled 'Bye Bye Venti,' as the narrator clarifies this was "Industries' response? To ridicule Mayor Michael Bloomberg as an overbearing nanny," adding that "The law was later overturned by the courts." An upbeat, almost circus-like instrumental music begins as a young boy is shown crying outdoors, with 'ConsumerFreedom.com' at the top. The U.S. Capitol building appears, followed by a protest scene outside where people are dumping processed foods like 'Healthy Choice' and 'Nabisco' into a trash can. A male voice, over the music, declares, "Everywhere you turn, somebody is telling us what we can't eat." A woman then speaks at a podium with two men beside her, with the Capitol in the background, as the female narrator explains that "advocates keep on trying... congressmen calling on the government to help consumers by demanding better labels, and at the very least recommending a daily limit for how much sugar is safe." The final segment focuses on a close-up of a 'Multi-Grain Cheerios' cereal box, highlighting the nutrition facts with red circles around 'Sodium 160 mg' (7%) and 'Sugars 6 g' (7%), while the narrator notes that "In Canada and the US, those limits exist for other ingredients like fat and sodium. Manufacturers must say what percentage of the recommended daily limit their product contains. But next to sugar..." emphasizing hidden sugar content in seemingly healthy foods.
